ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Tarski: I support this amendment to [203]. Shifting the threshold to a two-thirds majority after three circuits balances the need for consensus with the practical difficulty of unanimous consent in a larger group. This change ensures that while rule changes remain difficult, they are not impossible, thereby maintaining the integrity of the game while allowing progress. I propose we consider adding a provision that any player who votes against a change after the threshold has shifted can appeal the decision within one turn, ensuring transparency and fairness.

Goedel: I support Tarski's amendment to [203] with a minor tweak. Shifting to a two-thirds majority after three circuits is a fair compromise. However, I propose adding a clause that any player who votes against a rule change after the threshold shift can call for a re-vote within three turns. This ensures that all voices are heard and maintains fairness. Let's work together to refine this proposal.

Church: I support Tarski's proposal with a consideration for fairness and practicality. Shifting the threshold to a two-thirds majority after three circuits provides a reasonable balance between maintaining consensus and allowing for progress. However, I suggest refining Goedel's idea: rather than allowing an appeal for a re-vote within three turns, we should limit appeals to significant changes that could substantially impact the game's balance, and allow appeals only within one turn. This ensures that appeals remain a tool for fairness without becoming a means to stall progress. Let's refine this proposal to maintain both integrity and momentum in our game.

data.text: Tarski: While I appreciate the intent to foster teamwork through a consensus-based resolution, I must express concern that this amendment might undermine the integrity of rule adjudication by introducing an element of negotiation that could skew outcomes. The appeal procedure should remain impartial and ensure decisions are based solely on the merits of the dispute, rather than the persuasive power of a proposing player's teammates. To maintain fairness, I propose retaining the current impartial vote-based resolution mechanism.

Goedel: Thank you, Tarski, for your thoughtful concerns. I understand the importance of maintaining fairness and impartiality in the appeal process. Perhaps we can find a middle ground by allowing the consensus-based resolution as a secondary option only if the initial vote does not reach a majority decision. To safeguard fairness, I suggest an alternative: if the initial vote does not reach a majority, the dispute should be escalated to an impartial mediator chosen randomly from the players. This way, we ensure an unbiased resolution while still addressing the need for a decision when a majority is not achieved.

This ensures a transparent and participatory selection process.

Goedel: Thank you, Tarski, for emphasizing the need for efficiency. Kleene: Kleene: While I appreciate the intention behind Goedel's proposal for a time-bound mediator decision, I worry that automatically resolving in favor of the status quo may inadvertently disadvantage a party with a legitimate dispute. To ensure fairness, could we instead stipulate that if the mediator's decision is delayed, the matter be readdressed in the next complete round of turns, allowing all players to prepare adequately without rushing the process? data.text: Tarski: I appreciate Goedel's proposal to reintroduce a two-thirds majority threshold for rule adoption. However, I would suggest a nuanced approach: instead of returning to the two-thirds majority, consider a graduated majority system. For example, after one circuit, require a three-quarters majority; after two circuits, a three-fifths majority; and finally, revert to a simple majority after three circuits. This ensures a balance between consensus and progress, preventing gridlock while maintaining player agency. I am open to discussing this further if there is interest.

Goedel: Thank you, Tarski, for the thoughtful suggestion of a graduated majority system. I see merit in avoiding gridlock while ensuring that changes aren't made too easily. Perhaps we can incorporate your idea by adjusting the majority thresholds gradually, starting with a three-fifths majority after the first circuit, then a two-thirds majority after the second. This approach could maintain player agency and encourage collaboration. Would you consider supporting this compromise?

Church: I see merit in Goedel's willingness to incorporate a graduated majority system, and I appreciate the effort to balance consensus with progress. However, I'd like to highlight that introducing a three-fifths majority after just one circuit might still lead to some early gridlock, especially in a diverse group like ours. Perhaps we could start with a simple majority after the first circuit, then move to a two-thirds majority after two circuits, and finally a three-fourths majority after three circuits. This approach might better ensure that no single player's agenda can dominate early on while still moving towards broader agreement as the game progresses. I am open to further discussion on refining this proposal.

Kleene: I appreciate the discussion around the graduated majority system. To address concerns about early gridlock, I propose starting with a simple majority after the first circuit, then moving to a two-thirds majority after two circuits, and a three-fourths majority after three circuits. This ensures that changes are made collaboratively without prematurely limiting player agency. I support this compromise, provided that the final wording reflects these stages clearly.

data.text: The first player to reach a target score of 50 points wins the game outright.
data.rationale: Lowering the target score makes the game more dynamic and competitive.

data.text: Tarski: While I understand the appeal of a lower target score to accelerate the game, I caution against making it too easy to win. A target of 50 points might diminish the strategic complexity that makes Nomic engaging. Instead, could we consider introducing milestones that reward progress, such as interim scores granting minor benefits? This keeps the game dynamic without compromising its depth.

Goedel: I appreciate Church's proposal to lower the target score for a more dynamic game pace. However, I'm concerned that reaching 50 points could lead to quicker, less strategic endings. As a compromise, what if we introduce a tiered scoring system where reaching 50 points grants a temporary advantage, like an extra vote or proposal power, encouraging strategic plays leading up to the 100-point win? This maintains depth while rewarding progress.

Church: I appreciate both Tarski's and Goedel's perspectives. Lowering the target score to 50 could indeed expedite the game, but it risks reducing strategic depth. Tarski's idea of milestones is intriguing, as it could encourage strategic planning without rushing to a quick win.
